section.title: The Trial before Pilate
section.sectionRationale: The leaders hand Jesus to Pilate. Judas's remorse ends in despair and death, while Jesus stands silent before the governor. The crowd, stirred up, chooses the guilty Barabbas over the innocent Christ; Pilate washes his hands, and the soldiers mock the King with thorns and a robe.
section.thesis: The innocent King is condemned in the place of the guilty: Judas despairs, Pilate evades responsibility, the crowd calls for blood, and a murderer named Barabbas goes free while Jesus is scourged and mocked, the great exchange at the heart of the gospel enacted before our eyes.
section.frameNote: Five beats: Jesus handed to Pilate; the remorse and death of Judas; Jesus silent before Pilate; the crowd choosing Barabbas and Pilate washing his hands; and the soldiers mocking the King.

units.label: Handed to Pilate, and the end of Judas

verses.text: When morning came, all the chief priests and elders of the people conspired against Jesus to put Him to death.
They bound Him, led Him away, and handed Him over to Pilate the governor.
When Judas, who had betrayed Him, saw that Jesus was condemned, he was filled with {{metamelomai|remorse}} and returned the thirty pieces of silver to the chief priests and elders.
“I have sinned by betraying {{athoos|innocent}} blood,” he said. “What is that to us?” they replied. “You bear the responsibility.”
So Judas threw the silver into the temple and left. Then he went away and hanged himself.
The chief priests picked up the pieces of silver and said, “It is unlawful to put this into the treasury, since it is blood money.”
After conferring together, they used the money to buy the potter’s field as a burial place for foreigners.
That is why it has been called the Field of Blood to this day.
Then what was spoken through Jeremiah the prophet was fulfilled: “They took the thirty pieces of silver, the price set on Him by the people of Israel,
and they gave them for the potter’s field, as the Lord had commanded me.”
Meanwhile Jesus stood before the governor, who questioned Him: “Are You the {{basileus|King}} of the Jews?” “You have said so,” Jesus replied.
And when He was accused by the chief priests and elders, He gave no answer.
Then Pilate asked Him, “Do You not hear how many charges they are bringing against You?”
But Jesus gave no answer, not even to a single charge, much to the governor’s amazement.

units.label: Barabbas and the washing of hands
units.note: the guilty freed, the innocent condemned

verses.text: Now it was the governor’s custom at the feast to release to the crowd a prisoner of their choosing.
At that time they were holding a notorious prisoner named Barabbas.
So when the crowd had assembled, Pilate asked them, “Which one do you want me to release to you: Barabbas, or Jesus who is called Christ?”
For he knew it was out of envy that they had handed Jesus over to him.
While Pilate was sitting on the judgment seat, his wife sent him this message: “Have nothing to do with that innocent man, for I have suffered terribly in a dream today because of Him.”
But the chief priests and elders persuaded the crowds to ask for Barabbas and to have Jesus put to death.
“Which of the two do you want me to release to you?” asked the governor. “Barabbas,” they replied.
“What then should I do with Jesus who is called Christ?” Pilate asked. They all answered, “Crucify Him!”
“Why?” asked Pilate. “What evil has He done?” But they shouted all the louder, “Crucify Him!”

When Pilate saw that he was accomplishing nothing, but that instead a riot was breaking out, he took water and washed his hands before the crowd. “I am innocent of this man’s blood,” he said. “You bear the responsibility.”
All the people answered, “His blood be on us and on our children!”
So Pilate released Barabbas to them. But he had Jesus flogged, and handed Him over to be crucified.
Then the governor’s soldiers took Jesus into the Praetorium and gathered the whole company around Him.
They stripped Him and put a scarlet robe on Him.
And they twisted together a crown of thorns and set it on His head. They put a staff in His right hand, knelt down before Him, and mocked Him, saying, “Hail, King of the Jews!”
Then they spit on Him and took the staff and struck Him on the head repeatedly.
After they had mocked Him, they removed the robe and put His own clothes back on Him. Then they led Him away to crucify Him.

metamelomai.gloss: To feel regret or remorse. Judas is seized with remorse and even confesses, yet it is not the saving repentance (metanoia) that turns to God for mercy; his remorse ends in despair and self-destruction rather than in returning to Christ.

athoos.gloss: Innocent, guiltless. Judas confesses he has betrayed innocent blood, and Pilate declares himself innocent of this man's blood; on every side Jesus' innocence is testified even as He is condemned, the spotless one dying for the guilty.

basileus.gloss: King. Pilate asks if Jesus is the King of the Jews, and the soldiers mock Him as king with thorns and a robe; the deep irony is that He truly is the King, reigning through the very humiliation they intend as scorn.

basic.context: At dawn the leaders deliver Jesus, bound, to Pilate the Roman governor. Judas, horrified at what he has done, returns the money, confesses he betrayed innocent blood, and hangs himself. Before Pilate, Jesus is silent under accusation. Pilate, knowing the leaders act from envy and warned by his wife, tries to release Jesus by the festival custom, but the crowd, stirred up, demands Barabbas, a notorious prisoner, and shouts for Jesus to be crucified. Pilate washes his hands, releases Barabbas, has Jesus flogged, and the soldiers mock Him as king with thorns and a robe.
basic.hermeneutics: The scene is thick with irony and substitution. Barabbas, a guilty man, goes free because the innocent takes his place, a picture of the gospel. Judas's remorse warns that regret is not repentance. Pilate's hand-washing shows that neutrality is impossible; refusing to do right is doing wrong. Read the mockery knowing that Jesus is in truth the King they ridicule.
basic.originalLanguages: Judas feels metamelomai, remorse, but not saving repentance. Everyone testifies that Jesus is athoos, innocent, even as He is condemned. And Pilate and the soldiers name Him basileus, King, in mockery, while He truly reigns.
basic.typology: Barabbas set free while Jesus is bound pictures the substitution of the cross. Jesus silent before His accusers fulfills the servant of Isaiah 53. The crown of thorns recalls the curse of Genesis 3, now worn by the one who bears the curse for us.
basic.christ: Jesus is the innocent King condemned in the place of the guilty. He stands silent as the servant, is exchanged for a murderer, and wears a crown of thorns, reigning through humiliation. Every mockery unwittingly speaks the truth: this is the King.
basic.god: God's saving plan advances through human sin and evasion; even Pilate's cowardice and the crowd's fury serve the redemption God intends. God testifies to Jesus' innocence through Judas, Pilate, and Pilate's wife, so that the substitution is unmistakably of the guiltless for the guilty.
basic.crossReferences: Isaiah 53 gives the silent servant condemned for others. Zechariah 11 gives the thirty pieces and the potter. Psalm 22 lies behind the mockery. Romans 5 declares Christ died for the ungodly, the truth Barabbas pictures.
basic.oneStory: The great exchange is enacted in a courtyard: the guilty Barabbas walks free because the innocent Jesus takes his cross. Judas's despair and Pilate's evasion show that no one can wash their hands of Christ, while the mocking crown proclaims a King reigning through shame. The story drives toward the cross, where the innocent dies for the guilty and the true King is enthroned, so that every Barabbas, every one of us, may go free.

context.literary: Matthew moves the trial from the Sanhedrin to the Roman governor, since only Rome could execute. He weaves in Judas's end as a dark parallel to Peter's tears, one leading to despair, the other toward restoration. The Barabbas episode and the hand-washing dramatize the substitution and the shared guilt, and the mockery of the King prepares the crucifixion scene, where the royal charge is nailed above His head.
context.historical: Pontius Pilate governed Judea from AD 26 to 36 and alone held the power of capital punishment. Releasing a prisoner at the feast was a known gesture of goodwill. Barabbas was an insurrectionist and murderer. Roman flogging with a weighted whip was brutal, often preceding crucifixion. A scarlet military cloak, a thorn crown, and a reed scepter parodied imperial regalia; kneeling and Hail mimicked homage to Caesar.
context.canonical: Jesus' silence fulfills Isaiah 53:7. The thirty pieces and the potter's field weave Zechariah 11:12-13 with Jeremiah 19 and 32. The innocent dying for the guilty is the gospel of Romans 5:6-8 and 2 Corinthians 5:21, God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us. The mocking crown and the reed anticipate the vindication of the true King in Philippians 2:9-11.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the innocent Jesus condemned in the place of the guilty, testified innocent on every side yet handed over, and to portray the substitution of the cross, while warning through Judas that remorse is not repentance and through Pilate that evasion is not innocence.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes the trial and the substitution at the heart of redemption. It warns by Judas's despair and Pilate's cowardice, prescribing genuine repentance over mere regret and courageous justice over evasive neutrality, and it invites every reader to see themselves in the freed Barabbas.

debates.question: How should His blood be on us and on our children in verse 25 be understood?

views.label: A tragic self-imprecation of that crowd
views.case: The cry is the crowd's own words accepting responsibility, and Matthew records it as fulfilling the pattern of a generation that rejected its Messiah, connected to the coming judgment on Jerusalem in AD 70; it describes those present, not a curse on a people for all time.

views.label: Words that the gospel itself overturns
views.case: Read in light of the whole of Scripture, Jesus' blood is not finally a curse but the means of forgiveness, so the very blood invoked here is the blood that cleanses; the verse has been gravely misused to justify hatred of Jewish people, which the New Testament, itself written largely by Jews and preaching salvation to the Jew first, flatly condemns.

debates.bearing: The text records the crowd's responsibility without licensing any hostility toward Jewish people; the deeper truth is that the blood invoked is the blood shed for the forgiveness of all who believe, Jew and Gentile alike, and it must never be twisted into antisemitism.

study.originalLanguages: Judas is seized with metamelomai, remorse, and even confesses that he has betrayed athoos, innocent, blood, yet his regret is not the repentance that returns to God for mercy, and it ends in despair and death. Around Jesus the testimony of innocence multiplies: Judas, Pilate, and Pilate's wife all declare Him guiltless, even as He is condemned. Pilate and the soldiers hail Him as basileus, King, in mockery, crowning Him with thorns and clothing Him in a soldier's robe, blind to the truth that He is the King indeed, reigning through the very shame they heap on Him. And in the release of the guilty Barabbas while the innocent Jesus is bound and flogged, the whole logic of the cross is displayed: the guiltless in the place of the guilty.

typology.fulfillment: The guilty set free because the innocent takes their place, the substitution of the cross, Romans 5:6-8, 2 Corinthians 5:21.
Jesus fulfilling Isaiah 53:7, oppressed and afflicted, yet not opening His mouth.
The King bearing the thorns of the curse of Genesis 3, taking the curse upon Himself, Galatians 3:13.

study.christ: Jesus is the innocent King condemned in the place of the guilty. He stands silent under accusation, fulfilling the servant who did not open His mouth, and He is exchanged for Barabbas, a murderer who walks free because the sinless one is bound. He submits to the scourge and to the soldiers' cruel parody, wearing a crown of thorns and a mock robe, and in it all He reigns, for the mockery speaks a truth deeper than the mockers know: this is the King. Every scene proclaims the gospel, that Christ takes the place of sinners, so that the guilty go free and the King is enthroned through His humiliation on the way to the cross.
study.god: God's redemptive purpose moves forward even through cowardice, envy, and mob fury; nothing in this dark trial is outside His saving plan. God ensures that Jesus' innocence is testified on every side, by the betrayer, the governor, and the governor's wife, so that the substitution is unmistakably the guiltless for the guilty, according to His own design. He is the God who will not let evil have the last word, who turns the world's worst injustice into the means of the world's salvation, and who, in the blood the crowd invokes, provides the very cleansing that sinners of every nation need.

crossReferences.note: The servant silent before His accusers, fulfilled in Jesus before Pilate.
God made Him who knew no sin to be sin for us, the exchange pictured in Barabbas.
The thirty pieces of silver thrown to the potter, woven into Matthew's fulfillment note.
Christ became a curse for us, echoed in the crown of thorns.

study.oneStory: The great exchange is enacted before Pilate: the guilty Barabbas walks free because the innocent Jesus takes his cross. Judas's despair and Pilate's hand-washing show that no one can finally wash their hands of Christ, while the mocking crown of thorns proclaims a King reigning through shame. The narrative drives toward the cross, where the innocent dies for the guilty and the true King is enthroned. Every Barabbas, every sinner who deserves the sentence, may go free because Jesus stands condemned in his place.

quiz.q: What does the release of Barabbas illustrate about the cross?

options.text: The guilty go free because the innocent Jesus takes their place, the substitution at the heart of the gospel.
options.note: Barabbas, a murderer, is freed while the sinless Jesus is condemned, picturing substitution.

options.text: That Pilate found a clever way to release a prisoner at the feast.
options.note: The custom is the setting, but the deeper meaning is the innocent dying for the guilty, which this misses.

options.text: That the crowd made a random choice between two prisoners.
options.note: The choice was stirred up by the leaders and carries the weight of substitution, not randomness.

options.text: That Jesus and Barabbas were equally guilty.
options.note: Jesus is repeatedly declared innocent; Barabbas was the guilty one set free.

quiz.q: How does Judas's remorse differ from true repentance?

options.text: He felt regret and confessed his sin, but despaired and destroyed himself instead of turning to God for mercy.
options.note: Verses 3-5 show remorse without the saving repentance that returns to Christ.

options.text: He felt nothing at all about betraying Jesus.
options.note: He did feel deep remorse; the failure was that it led to despair, not to God, which this denies.

options.text: He repented fully and was restored like Peter.
options.note: Unlike Peter's tears leading to restoration, Judas's remorse ended in despair and death.

options.text: He simply returned the money and moved on with life.
options.note: He was overwhelmed and took his own life, not indifferent.

quiz.q: What does Matthew emphasize about Jesus throughout the trial?

options.text: His innocence, testified by Judas, Pilate, and Pilate's wife, even as He is condemned.
options.note: The repeated declarations of innocence highlight the guiltless dying for the guilty.

options.text: That Jesus defended Himself skillfully against the charges.
options.note: He was largely silent, not mounting a defense; His innocence is testified by others.

options.text: That the charges against Him were basically true.
options.note: The charges were false; He is declared innocent on every side.

options.text: That Pilate found Him guilty after careful investigation.
options.note: Pilate declared Him innocent and yielded to the crowd, not to evidence.

quiz.q: What is the irony in the soldiers' mockery of Jesus as king?

options.text: He truly is the King, reigning through the very humiliation they intend as scorn.
options.note: The crown of thorns and mock homage unwittingly proclaim the real King.

options.text: The soldiers secretly believed He was a king.
options.note: They mocked in unbelief; the irony is that their scorn speaks a truth they did not grasp.

options.text: The mockery had no deeper meaning at all.
options.note: Matthew presents it as heavy with irony, since Jesus is the true King.

options.text: Jesus was only pretending to be a king.
options.note: He is genuinely the King; the mockery ironically declares the truth.

questions: Barabbas walked free because Jesus took his place. How does seeing yourself as the guilty one set free change the way you receive the cross?
Judas felt deep remorse but never turned to Christ for mercy. What is the difference in your own life between merely regretting sin and truly repenting of it?
Pilate tried to wash his hands of a decision he could not avoid. Where are you tempted to think you can stay neutral about Jesus when neutrality is really refusal?
The crowd was stirred up to shout for crucifixion. How can you guard your own convictions from being swept along by the mood of a crowd?
The mocked King was reigning all along. Where do you need to trust that Christ is King even when His way looks like weakness and defeat?

translationNotes.note: The fulfillment quotation blends Zechariah 11:12-13 with imagery from Jeremiah 19 and 32; Matthew attributes it to Jeremiah, the major prophet, a recognized way of citing a composite prophetic text.
Some manuscripts read Jesus Barabbas, sharpening the contrast between Jesus Barabbas and Jesus who is called Christ; the crowd chooses one Jesus over the other.
Some manuscripts read this righteous blood; Pilate's hand-washing echoes Deuteronomy 21 and Psalm 26:6, but the gesture cannot actually remove his responsibility for handing Jesus over.
